transcript.text: The future of religion.
I think that's an interesting thing to think about these days.
Like, what's going to happen with religion?
I know clergy numbers and Catholicism are dropping a lot.
Catholicism is getting smaller and breaking up.
Islam is growing.
Maybe that's because there's a resurgence in, like, you know, black pride.
And, you know, Buddhism is growing.
But I think a lot of religions have a tendency to think that their religion is the religion of the future.
And their philosophy is the philosophy of the future.
And it's like, in the future, they're going to be right and everybody else is going to be wrong.
And I don't really see it that way because I think everybody in the world, every idea, has their own representation of the truth, which is the truth.
And, you know, in the times, the day and age we're in now, with the whole growth of knowledge, where we're going to figure it all out pretty soon.
We're all figuring it all out now, age of information.
That all the best parts of each religion are going to rise to the top.
And all the crap is just going to fall by the wayside.
And to the point where every religion is going to feel like they win and there's going to be no losers because it's just going to take the best part of every religion.
And, like, nowadays, fundamentalist Christians are starting to believe, you know, whereas only 10 years ago, they didn't believe in astrology.
And now they're starting to believe in astrology because it's starting to be backed up by science with experiments.
You know, and believe in even reincarnation and lots of concepts that were just strictly for other religions before.
Only they're still feeling like they're right because God concept is still growing strong and, you know, with all these, like, non-locality experiments and, like, double-slit experiment.
And, like, I think there's going to be winners and no losers.
I think, like, Buddhists will finally start believing in God and then just say that they'll have always kind of believed it.
They just had a different idea, you know.
As far as organization of the future of religion, some people might say, oh, but it's all going to, like, like, New Age, I think.
Maybe I should do a video just on New Age.
New Age, I think, is the perfect representation of the spirituality of the future.
I would consider it a religion, but it's, like, it's not organized at all.
It kind of takes the best of every religion.
But I think, basically, that's what most people will be in the future.
Well, in the future future, like, utopia future, maybe, you know, not within our lifetimes, but maybe a little longer than that.
But everybody will be, like, New Age, where everybody pretty much agrees on the same thing and there's just no organization.
It's just groups of people who kind of, you know, loosely knit.
But as far as the religions are today, like, some people might say that they're going to completely evaporate, dissolve, and just fall by the wayside.
And I don't think that's going to happen.
People have a tendency to really cherish legacy.
You know, like, the Catholic Church is the legacy of the Roman Empire.
And I think the Roman Empire stayed strong so long, but just because on its legacy, on its reputation, people like to see something.
I mean, not just from them protecting trade routes, but they just wanted to see that great Roman symbol, you know, and all the statues and everything just go on.
You know, like how the royal family in England keeps going, because people just like to hang on to that.
It's their little vestige from the past that they like to cherish.
And I think all religions will be like that.
People will hang on to their religion.
They'll be Catholics forever.
They'll just be like New Age Catholics.
Catholics that believe the same as New Agers, but they just stick to their rites and rituals.
The Jews will stick to their rituals and stuff because they've done it forever.
And it'll just morph into something that's contemporary and, you know, appropriate for what we're doing, what we believe now.
So, I mean, words change their meaning to morph into what you mean.
Like, I think the N-word might even eventually mean friends, because black people use it so much as that.
Words change a lot.
Like, 500 years ago, girl actually meant boy.
You know, then it changed, like it was boy, and then it changed to like young boy, and then, yeah, now it's girl.
So, but, I mean, so like, you know, the rituals might, if they're bad, like sacrifice was always something that they stopped.
Well, that's a whole other conversation.
But anyways, yeah, I think they'll always be religions.
People will just adopt, adopt it, you know, maybe even if it's totally different.
So, they'll always be a, you know, Amish.
Maybe people will stop not driving cars.
They'll all be driving, in a hundred years, they'll all be driving cars and having houses and that have all the amenities or whatever.
And they'll still call themselves Amish because they like to keep that community together.
The bloodlines and the friends and just the good stuff.
But basically, my main point of this video, I think I got sidetracked a lot, is that there's not going to be no losers in the future of religion.
All the religions, even the fundamentalist Christians to the fundamentalist Muslims, are just going to morph.
And just like one day, they're just going to be like, oh yeah, no, I believe that.
I believe in reincarnation and, but I'm still a Muslim, you know.
So, but I think, yeah, in the future there will be like one world religion.
Everybody will believe the same thing.
Everybody will agree.
It'll be like science.
Everybody knows the world's flat.
Everybody knows the moon's out there and it's so-and-so miles away.
Well, I think religious concepts, everybody will just know it.
Everybody, and so they'll, and at that point, there'll be no more actual religion.
And that point may actually come really soon.
But, you know, little rituals people will still do just for fun.
So, anyways, the future of religion.
